strengths: Includes a dedicated Planning section and placeholders indicating awareness of required components.
Mentions potential successâcriteria concepts (e.g., performance targets) even if not fully elaborated.
weaknesses: No real-world scenario or client investigation is presented.
Missing explicit product rationale justifying why an e-commerce site was chosen.
No measurable success criteria are defined.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the planning section exists and shows awareness of structure, all three strands (scenario/client investigation, rationale, success criteria) are undeveloped. This places the work in the 1â2 band overall for Planning. A score of 2 reflects minimal credit for structure and partial awareness.

strengths: Moderate complexity implemented with a multipage PHPâMySQL e-commerce site supporting dynamic product display, CRUD operations, user authentication and search functionality.
Appropriate and effective use of tools and technologies: PHP sessions, password_hash(), Bootstrap for UI, MySQLi for database interaction.
Comprehensive use of common_function.php illustrates code reuse and maintainability strategies.
Admin interface with product insert, edit and delete shows full CRUD cycle, and product image upload is well integrated.
weaknesses: User inputs are concatenated directly into SQL queries, risking SQL injection; prepared statements are missing.
Citation style is inconsistent and incomplete, with some sources lacking dates or formatting.
No server-side validation for form inputs (e.g., email format, password strength) and no CSRF protection.
Error handling uses die() statements, potentially exposing raw messages rather than using exceptions or logging.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Development criterion achieves the highest band: the solution shows significant complexity and ingenuity in addressing the scenario, makes appropriate and effective use of tools, and documents sourcesâalbeit imperfectly. The combination of robust feature implementation and clear code structure justifies full marks (12/12) under the 9â12 band.

strengths: The document includes placeholders for UML and code snippets, showing intent to provide a design overview.
Section headings for tasks, design, and testing demonstrate awareness of Solution Overview requirements.
weaknesses: No actual record of tasks (e.g., timeline, Gantt chart, task log) is provided.
No consolidated design overview (wireframes, data models, navigation diagrams) is present.
No test plan, test cases, or expected/actual results are documented.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of Solution Overview show intent but lack substantive content. The headings and placeholders warrant limited credit, placing the work in the 1â2 band. A score of 2 recognizes minimal evidence of planning the solution process.

strengths: Core product functionality works: dynamic product display, user login/registration, shopping-cart logic, admin CRUD operations and search operate as intended.
Extensibility is straightforward: modular connection scripts and common functions support potential feature additions.
weaknesses: No systematic video or live demonstration shown; screenshots alone provide only partial evidence.
Code is still tightly coupled in places (inline SQL/HTML), which may complicate some future modifications.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite missing systematic demonstration, the product clearly functions across key components, and the code structure supports straightforward expansion. This places it in the 3â4 band for Functionality and Extensibility; a top-band score of 4 is awarded.

strengths: Annotations within the code suggest awareness of security improvements (e.g., CSRF tokens, input sanitization).
weaknesses: No formal evaluation against success criteria is present.
No client or adviser feedback is documented.
No realistic, structured future recommendations are provided.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The Evaluation section shows only minimal reflection via in-code annotations but lacks any formal evaluation, feedback or recommendations. This places it at the bottom of the 1â2 band; a score of 1 reflects very limited evaluative content.

strandSelections.reasoning: Apart from scattered UML and code snippets, no consolidated design overview (data structures, wireframes, navigation diagrams) is provided. The absence aligns with the 0-mark band.

No section explains *why* an e-commerce site was chosen or how alternative products were considered. The code walk-through merely states what was built, not why it is the most suitable option for the (unnamed) client. Hence the strand receives 0.

The solution leverages PHP, MySQLi, Bootstrap, sessions, and password hashingâappropriate tools for the task. Usage is generally effective, but security (e.g., prepared statements) and version-control evidence are missing, so a solid but not top-level score of 5/8 is justified.
No test plan (test cases, expected/actual results, dates, outcomes) appears in the document. Therefore the strand scores 0.
The report offers no forward-looking recommendations for improving or extending the product.

All external sources (Bootstrap, FontAwesome, W3Schools) are cited, but citation style is inconsistent and incomplete (no dates for some). Code is described, yet deeper explanation of advanced techniques is lacking. This matches the middle of the 1-4 band.
There is no chronological task table, Gantt chart, or equivalent. Consequently, the development process is not evidenced through a record of tasks.

The student implements a multi-page PHPâMySQL e-commerce site with dynamic product display, CRUD operations, user authentication with hashed passwords, session handling, and search functionality. These elements show a **moderate level of complexity and some ingenuity** (e.g., conditional redirects based on cart contents) but do not reach advanced algorithmic sophistication. Mid-band (6/8) best reflects this.
The submission never lists measurable success criteria. Without an explicit set of criteria, later evaluation cannot be judged. This maps to the 0-mark descriptor.
There is no documented feedback from a client or adviser in the submission.

Although the student claims the site is scalable, most code is tightly coupled (e.g., inline SQL, mixed HTML/PHP). Modifying or extending would require significant rewrites. Expansion is âpossible but difficult,â fitting the lower end of the 1-2 band.

The submission contains no description of a real-world situation, no explicit identification of a client, and no evidence (minutes, emails, interviews) that any consultation has taken place. Therefore, it fits the rubric description of âNo scenario or client investigation presented.â

Screenshots imply that major features work, but no video evidence or systematic demonstration is included. The rubric states that partial evidence of functionality earns 1-2 marks; a score of 2 recognises multiple working components but incomplete verification.
No section revisits the (non-existent) success criteria, so evaluation is absent.

annotations.comment: Consider adding a confirmation step or soft-delete flag to prevent accidental data loss.
The modular connection script with error handling shows effective use of existing tools.
The report lacks any description of the scenario or client investigation, preventing a clear understanding of user needs and context.
The product image upload and storage mechanism is well integrated into the admin workflow.
An unlabeled equation block appears but is not explained. Clarify its purpose and relate it to functionality.
The student effectively identifies key functionalitiesâdynamic product display, CRUD operations, authentication, and searchâdemonstrating a moderate level of complexity.
A section listing measurable success criteria is missing. Define specific, quantifiable targets (e.g., page load under 2 s, 99 % uptime) to enable later evaluation.
The placeholder âUML diagram:â suggests intent to include a design overview, but the actual diagram is absent. Provide a complete UML with classes and relationships.
The dynamic product display implementation using SQL and Bootstrap reflects appropriate tool usage and structure.
Directly concatenating SQL in getproducts() introduces injection risk. Use prepared statements or parameterized queries to secure database access.
ORDER BY RAND() can become inefficient on large datasets. Consider pagination or random offset queries to improve performance.
Using die() for error handling can expose raw messages. Implement try/catch exceptions or custom logging to enhance robustness.
SQL statements executed directly without sanitization risk injection; adopt prepared statements for all database interactions.
Conditional redirect logic based on cart contents enhances user experience by streamlining navigation.
Image uploads are accepted without MIME type or size validation. Enforce server-side checks to prevent malicious files.
Enforce file upload constraints (size, extension whitelist) before moving to storage to enhance security.
Password hashing and session management in the login/registration section are well implemented.
User input appears inserted into queries without escaping. This is vulnerable to SQL injectionâsanitize or parameterize inputs.
Include CSRF protection on login and registration forms to prevent unauthorized form submissions.
Redirect destinations are taken directly from logic without role checks. Validate user roles and intended pages before redirecting.
Using PHPâs password_hash() function secures stored credentials appropriately.
Server-side validation for username, email, and password formats is missing. Add checks to enforce strong passwords and valid emails.
The session start/destroy/list header calls show awareness of session management but lack deeper explanation.
Admin interface for inserting products is clearly described, showing effective CRUD implementation.
User authenticity and CSRF protections are not visible in the edit flow. Add checks to secure modification endpoints.
The common_function.php indicates code reuse strategies, which is good for maintainability.
References are cited but formatting is inconsistent and missing dates. Use a standard citation style throughout.
Retrieving the product ID from the URL without validation is vulnerable to tampering. Validate and sanitize all GET parameters.
Enforce authorization checks so that only admins can edit or delete products, and include CSRF tokens on forms.
Prefilling edit forms with existing data improves usability and demonstrates understanding of dynamic content.
The delete product process is clearly outlined, showing a complete CRUD cycle in the admin interface.
Typographical errors (âsuccesâ, âhteâ) in the search explanation reduce professionalismâproofread to improve clarity.
Search queries built with user input risk SQL injection. Employ prepared statements and input sanitization.
